entries.title: The Sidewalk Between Street and Building
entries.rawOfficialText: Dubai barely has them; in New York they’re bustling with people and halal food stands. Research the history of the sidewalk - that liminal gap between street and building that serves as a gathering place for some and a bike path for others. Where did they first emerge - and when were the first modern sidewalks built? How different are they from place to place?
entries.studentExplanation:
A sidewalk may look ordinary, but it is actually a designed threshold between the moving street and the built world. In ancient places such as Pompeii, raised pavements and stepping stones helped pedestrians move safely beside dirty, wet, or cart-filled streets. In modern cities, sidewalks can become lively public rooms full of food stands, conversation, waiting, cycling, and informal exchange. But when sidewalks are too narrow, interrupted, or missing, the city quietly tells pedestrians that they are less important than cars or buildings.

The source notes add historical texture: ancient cities had raised pedestrian spaces, medieval streets often mixed walkers and carts, Pont Neuf helped normalize elegant sidewalks, and post-fire London regulation pushed paving and bollards. Sidewalks are safety, hygiene, commerce, and social life at once. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see that sidewalks are not leftover pavement but civic infrastructure. At the section level, it opens the guiding section by showing that liminal spaces can be ordinary and still powerful. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds and journeys because every trip through a city begins by deciding who gets safe, comfortable space to move and pause.
entries.takeaway: Sidewalks reveal how cities distribute safety, movement, and public life
examples: in New York they’re bustling with people and halal food stands
Where did they first emerge - and when were the first modern sidewalks built
How different are they from place to place
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether sidewalks should be treated mainly as movement infrastructure or as shared civic space.
entries.counterargument: Some sidewalks are so narrow, broken, or neglected that treating them as rich civic spaces can sound idealized rather than realistic.

visualSections.title: The Sidewalk Between Street and Building

items.title: A historical threshold
items.note: Raised sidewalks and stepping stones in Pompeii show that cities have long separated pedestrian movement from street traffic and dirty runoff.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch to find a real photograph of raised sidewalks and stepping stones in ancient Pompeii. Use a reliable source such as an archaeological, educational, travel-history, or Wikimedia/official Pompeii source. This is a real historical feature, so do not use /imagecreator or /imagegen unless only a non-final explanatory diagram is needed. The image must show the raised pavement and/or stepping stones clearly.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum or shorter, explaining streets, pavements, or urban life in Pompeii. If the video is too broad, use it only as a LINK button.
items.imageLicense: Exact or source-preserved visual from the app/source guidance

entries.title: Why Moving Sidewalks Never Took Over the World
entries.rawOfficialText: In the once-futuristic world of the Jetsons, the sidewalks don’t just sit there: they whisk people along to their destinations. Something like this still happens at many airports and even some amusement parks. Learn more about historical efforts to popularize moving sidewalks. Discuss with your team: why didn’t they catch on more widely? Where would you install them today, if you could?
entries.studentExplanation:
Moving sidewalks feel like a perfect future: instead of walking, people glide toward their destination. But the idea is much older than The Jetsons. Nineteenth- and twentieth-century inventors imagined movable pavement for fairs, cities, stadiums, and subway systems. The 1893 Chicago World’s Columbian Exposition even had an early moving sidewalk on a pier. The reason the idea never took over ordinary streets is not that it was fake. It is that it worked best in special, controlled environments, such as airports, amusement parks, and exhibitions, where movement is predictable and maintenance is worth the cost.

Moving sidewalks failed as ordinary urban infrastructure because they need controlled flow, maintenance, predictable entry and exit points, and enough demand to justify cost. That is why they survive better in airports, exhibitions, and amusement parks than on every city street. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students distinguish a niche success from a universal transformation. At the section level, it shows that futuristic infrastructure survives only when it matches real-world conditions. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress and prediction because not every “almost obvious” invention becomes everyday life.
entries.takeaway: Progress depends on fit between technology, place, cost, and human behavior
examples: Something like this still happens at many airports and even some amusement parks
Learn more about historical efforts to popularize moving sidewalks
Discuss with your team: why didn’t they catch on more widely
Where would you install them today, if you could
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating why some futuristic inventions remain niche even when they technically work.
entries.counterargument: A design can stay niche not because it failed in principle, but because it only makes sense in very specific environments.

visualSections.title: Why Moving Sidewalks Never Took Over the World

items.title: Before The Jetsons
items.note: Moving sidewalks were imagined and built long before cartoon futurism made them famous.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch to find a real historical image of the moving sidewalk at the 1893 World’s Columbian Exposition in Chicago, ideally the pier image cited by Smithsonian/Wikimedia/Guinness. This is a real historical image, so do not generate.

items.title: The airport niche
items.note: Moving sidewalks succeed where movement is linear, predictable, and heavy enough to justify maintenance.

If no suitable image is found, /imagecreator may create a realistic WSC-style airport walkway scene as fallback.

items.title: Future idea, limited use
items.note: The key question is not whether the technology works, but where it actually makes sense.

entries.title: How Parking Took Over the Landscape
entries.rawOfficialText: Many sidewalks have cars parked alongside them. There are at least a billion parking spots in the United States alone - three times as many as there are people. With your team, investigate the history of parking, then discuss with your team: should people have a right to free parking at their homes and places of work?

links.label: History of parking - NPR / Fresh Air

entries.studentExplanation:
Parking can look like a small personal convenience, but at city scale it becomes a huge design system. When a society builds enough spaces to store cars at homes, jobs, shops, stadiums, and streets, it quietly reshapes land values, sidewalks, housing, traffic, and expectations. The key question is not just “Where should drivers park?” It is “Who pays for parking when it is called free, and what else could that land have become?”

The added frame is land use. “Free” parking still costs land, housing, walking space, taxes, and urban attention; the real question is what a city stops becoming when so much of it is reserved for cars waiting between trips. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see parking as a policy choice rather than a natural right. At the section level, it connects sidewalks to car storage and public space. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds because parking lots and curb spaces are in-between places: not destinations, but powerful structures that shape how every journey begins and ends.
entries.takeaway: Parking is a design and policy choice that reshapes public expectations and urban form
examples: Many sidewalks have cars parked alongside them
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether free parking is a fair right, a hidden subsidy, or a habit created by car-centered design.
entries.counterargument: In low-density places, large amounts of parking can still feel practically necessary rather than ideologically excessive.

visualSections.title: How Parking Took Over the Landscape

items.title: A billion parking spaces
items.note: Parking is presented as a vast land-use system, not just a few spaces beside a sidewalk.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch to find a real aerial or street-level image showing the scale of parking in the United States, such as large surface lots or curb parking. If no single image communicates the idea clearly, use /imagecreator to make a realistic WSC app-style infographic showing people, cars, and land area, but do not make it cartoon/anime. WSC app mandatory.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video, 5-7 minutes maximum, about parking policy, parking minimums, or Henry Grabar’s Paved Paradise. If only long interviews exist, use the NPR link as a LINK button.

links.label: History of parking - NPR / Fresh Air

items.title: Free parking is not free
items.note: The hidden trade-off is land use: parking occupies space that could support housing, parks, shops, sidewalks, or transit.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagecreator to create a realistic WSC app-style split visual: one side shows land used for parked cars; the other shows possible alternate uses such as housing, trees, sidewalks, or shops. Use /imagesearch first only if a clean real comparison image exists.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational explainer on parking minimums or the hidden cost of free parking.

links.label: History of parking - NPR / Fresh Air

items.title: Parking beside the sidewalk
items.note: Parking directly changes the pedestrian experience and the use of curb space.

entries.title: When Parking Lots Become Architecture
entries.rawOfficialText:
People usually park their cars and then dash on to their destination as quickly as they can; they may not give the parking lot itself a second glance. Liminal spaces are easy to overlook on your way to somewhere else. But some parking lots are self-conscious architectural masterpieces. Read about the Temple Street Parking Garage in New Haven - and then look into these other lots below. Should more parking lots be built with this much architectural flourish, or should they be as invisible as possible?

Lego Parking House | Billund, Denmark
Parc des Celestins | Lyon, France
Michigan Theater | Detroit, Michigan
Garagenatelier Car Park | Herdern, Switzerland
9th Avenue Parkade | Calgary, Canada
entries.studentExplanation:
Parking structures are usually treated as places to pass through quickly, but some are designed as serious architecture. Temple Street Parking Garage makes car movement visible in concrete; Lego Parking House turns a garage into brand-based urban design; Parc des Celestins hides a visual artwork inside an underground garage; Michigan Theater turns a former movie palace into a haunting parking space; Garagenatelier embeds garages into a Swiss hillside; and Calgary’s 9th Avenue Parkade was designed so it could one day adapt to other uses. Together, they show that even spaces meant for temporary storage can carry meaning, memory, and civic identity.

Use the named garages as a design spectrum: Temple Street exposes movement, Lego Parking House turns storage into brand play, Parc des Celestins hides an artwork underground, Michigan Theater preserves a ghost of old entertainment, and Calgary designs for possible future conversion. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students rethink functional spaces they normally ignore. At the section level, it deepens the idea that liminal spaces can be designed, not just tolerated. At the year-theme level, it connects to thresholds because a parking garage is usually between one place and another, yet it can still become a destination of attention.
entries.takeaway: Even in-between utility spaces can carry beauty, memory, identity, and future possibility
examples: they may not give the parking lot itself a second glance
Liminal spaces are easy to overlook on your way to somewhere else
But some parking lots are self-conscious architectural masterpieces
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether utility spaces should remain invisible or deserve beauty, atmosphere, and civic ambition.
entries.counterargument: Design effort spent on garages can look misguided if a city has more urgent social or infrastructure needs competing for the same resources.

items.title: Temple Street Parking Garage
items.note: Paul Rudolph’s New Haven garage treats car circulation and concrete structure as an architectural statement.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch to find real images of Paul Rudolph’s Temple Street Parking Garage in New Haven. Prefer Hidden Architecture, Paul Rudolph Institute, or other architecture archives. This is a named building, so do not use /imagecreator or /imagegen.

entries.title: What Is a Snout House?
entries.rawOfficialText: Many suburban homes don’t hide their parking lots at all - their garages are front-and-center. Consider these so-called “snout houses”, then discuss with your team: have you seen any in your community, and would it be better if their garages were more hidden?
entries.studentExplanation:
A snout house is a suburban home where the garage projects toward the street and becomes the most visible part of the front. Critics dislike this because the house seems to announce the needs of the car before the life of the people inside. But the debate is not one-sided: on small lots, a front-facing garage can be practical, affordable, and efficient. The real question is what a neighborhood communicates when the most public face of home is built around storage for vehicles.

A snout house is a small architectural argument about priorities. When the garage dominates the street face, the home tells neighbors and pedestrians that car storage is the public front; the defense is practicality on small lots, but the cost is weaker street life. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students see that a home’s front design carries social meaning. At the section level, it moves the sidewalk discussion into suburban domestic architecture. At the year-theme level, it connects to home and thresholds because the garage can become the real “front door” of car-centered life.
entries.takeaway: Domestic architecture can reveal whether a neighborhood is organized around cars or human street life
examples: The garage as the face of the house
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether suburban design should privilege cars, curb appeal, neighborhood life, or a balance among them.
entries.counterargument: A prominent garage can be a practical response to everyday life rather than proof that a neighborhood has failed socially or aesthetically.

items.title: The garage as the face of the house
items.note: A front-dominant garage changes what a home presents to the street.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch first to find a real, sourceable photo of a snout house or front-dominant garage house, ideally from the Jalopnik article or a related urban design source. If no perfect image is available and the slide is illustrating the general concept, use /imagecreator to create a realistic, non-anime WSC app-style suburban facade where the garage clearly projects forward.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video about car-centered suburban design, snout houses, or garage-dominated suburbs, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum.
items.imageLicense: Exact or source-preserved visual from the app/source guidance

items.title: Car convenience versus street life
items.note: The debate centers on a snout house can be practical for the owner while still weakening the street-facing human experience.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagecreator to create a WSC app-style comparison diagram: one facade with a dominant garage, one facade with porch/windows facing the street. It can be a clean diagram/illustration, not necessarily photorealistic, but must match the WSC app aesthetic and avoid anime/human characters.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short video on suburban street design, garages, or walkable neighborhoods.

channelVideos.title: Why Did Garages Take Over the American Home?

entries.title: Frank Lloyd Wright and the House Built Around the Car
entries.rawOfficialText: Modern architect Frank Lloyd Wright would certainly have hidden them: he imagined cars living inside of houses. For him, there was no need for wasted liminal space between homes and streets. Explore the design and history of his famous Robie House, then discuss with your team: does it truly “blur the boundaries between interior space and the world of nature”? How different would your school look if it had followed the same principles?

links.label: Robie House - Frank Lloyd Wright Trust

entries.studentExplanation:
Frank Lloyd Wright did not imagine the car as something that should simply shout from the front of the house. He wanted modern homes to absorb movement, landscape, and daily life into a more unified design. Robie House is important because it shows Wright’s Prairie-style idea of a building that stretches horizontally, connects interior and exterior, and feels rooted in its site. In this entry, the car is only part of the bigger question: should a home be a hard box separated from street and nature, or a flowing threshold between them?

Wright’s carport idea matters because he did not simply bolt a garage onto a house. He tried to integrate movement, shelter, landscape, and daily life, so the car became part of a wider architectural flow rather than the loudest object on the facade. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this gives students a design alternative to the snout house. At the section level, it connects cars, garages, homes, sidewalks, and landscape into one architectural problem. At the year-theme level, it connects to liminality and thresholds because Wright’s work asks where the inside ends and the outside begins.
entries.takeaway: Architecture can redesign thresholds between home, street, movement, and landscape
examples: For him, there was no need for wasted liminal space between homes and streets
How different would your school look if it had followed the same principles
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating whether architecture should blur inside and outside or keep boundaries clear for privacy and function.
entries.counterargument: Soft boundaries can feel beautiful and humane, but they can also weaken privacy, clarity, and practical separation.

visualSections.title: Frank Lloyd Wright and the House Built Around the Car

items.title: Cars inside the architectural order
items.note: Wright’s response to cars was integration, not front-facing garage dominance.

If no direct image is available, use an exact Wright car/house related source image rather than generating a fake Wright design.

entries.title: Turning Parking Into Public Space
entries.rawOfficialText: Landscape architects RIOS transformed one set of downtown parking lots into a new community gathering place, then discuss with your team: are there places in your own city that should be converted in a similar way?

links.label: Grand Park - The Park for Everyone by RIOS

entries.studentExplanation:
Grand Park in Los Angeles shows that parking-dominated land does not have to stay that way forever. RIOS transformed a large downtown area once filled with parking lots into a civic park with lawns, fountains, gardens, a playground, walking loops, and bright public furniture. The project matters because it changes the role of the space: instead of storing cars between destinations, the land becomes a destination for public life.

Grand Park shows conversion, not decoration. A parking-dominated civic zone became lawns, paths, fountains, bright furniture, gardens, and gathering space, proving that land once used only between destinations can become a destination itself. Use this to read infrastructure as a design choice: what space is being created, where pedestrians or cars are placed, how movement changes, and who gains or loses from that layout.
entries.whyItMatters: At the entry level, this helps students imagine urban land as changeable. At the section level, it gives the section a human-centered conclusion after sidewalks, parking, garages, and car-focused houses. At the year-theme level, it connects to progress and liminality because a place people once passed through can become a place where they arrive, gather, and belong.
entries.takeaway: Cities can redefine overlooked car-centered land as shared human-centered space
examples: From parking lots to The Park for Everyone
Design details that invite people in
entries.debateRelevance: Useful when debating how cities should reassign land and whether public gathering places can be worth more than car storage.
entries.counterargument: Replacing parking can create real mobility trade-offs, so conversion works best when access, circulation, and local needs are considered seriously.

items.title: From parking lots to The Park for Everyone
items.note: Grand Park appears as a concrete example of reclaiming car-centered land for public life.
items.imageGuidance: Use /imagesearch to find real images of Gloria Molina Grand Park / Grand Park Los Angeles by RIOS, preferably from RIOS or Landezine. This is a real named landscape project, so do not generate.
items.videoGuidance: Use /youtubesearch to find a short educational video about Grand Park Los Angeles, RIOS Grand Park, or urban parking-to-park transformation, ideally 5-7 minutes maximum.
